behaviors.description: The wait time between retries doubles with each consecutive failure, up to a configured maximum.
behaviors.justification: While the backoff formula (2^N × base, capped at max) is mathematical, a real failure isn't just "the formula is wrong"—it's that the actual async retry loop doesn't apply the backoff correctly or the implementation forgets to await the sleep. A Unit test mocking time could hide if sleep is never awaited, or if the retry loop doesn't integrate the calculated wait correctly. Integration testing the backoff within the actual async retry cycle is needed to catch real implementation bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test scenario with multiple consecutive rate-limit responses. Verify that wait time between retries follows the pattern: base, 2×base, 4×base, ..., up to max. Use a controlled mock clock or mocked Timer to avoid actual delays, but verify the calculated times are passed to the actual sleep/delay mechanism and that retries occur after each calculated wait.

behaviors.description: The retry logic correctly identifies a rate-limit response and computes the appropriate wait time before the next attempt.
behaviors.justification: This is response parsing and detection logic. The real failure modes are: (a) not detecting 429 status, (b) not reading Retry-After header, (c) extracting the wrong wait time. These are all testable with mock response objects at Unit level. No I/O or async machinery is involved—only parsing logic.
behaviors.test_description: Create mock HTTP response objects with various rate-limit indicators (status 429, Retry-After header with seconds/date, etc.) and verify the detection logic correctly identifies them. Test edge cases: missing header (use default backoff), malformed header, negative/zero wait times. Verify the extracted wait time is correct.

behaviors.description: After the wait, the client actually resends the original request and returns the successful result to the caller transparently.
behaviors.justification: A Unit test with mocked HTTP could verify "the logic tries to resend." But the realistic failure is that the actual async retry chain doesn't work end-to-end: the original request isn't actually resent, or the result isn't properly awaited and returned to the caller. Testing the full async cycle—wait, resend, return—requires Integration-level testing to catch promise-chain bugs, missing awaits, or improper result propagation.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the HTTP client to return a rate-limit response on the first call and a successful response on the second. Call the async function and await the result. Verify: (1) two requests were made in order, (2) the final returned result matches the successful response, (3) the caller doesn't see the intermediate rate-limit error—only the final success.

behaviors.description: After the configured maximum number of retries, the client returns a typed `QuotaExhaustedError` rather than a generic error.
behaviors.justification: This is error-handling logic: after N retries, throw a specific error type. The real failure is returning the wrong error type (generic Error, network error, etc.) instead of QuotaExhaustedError. This is testable at Unit level by mocking the HTTP client to always return rate-limit responses and verifying the thrown error type.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the HTTP client to always return 429 rate-limit responses. Call the async function with a low max-retry count (e.g., 3). Verify the thrown error is an instance of QuotaExhaustedError, has a sensible message, and is not a generic or network error.

behaviors.description: The full retry chain—initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking—terminates correctly and does not loop indefinitely before surfacing the error.
behaviors.justification: A Unit test could verify "max retry logic is enforced." But the realistic failure is that the actual async promise chain hangs, loops infinitely, or fails to surface the error. This requires running the actual async loop and verifying it completes within an expected time. A Unit test mocking individual pieces could pass while the real async machinery hangs.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test scenario where the client would loop indefinitely if max-retry enforcement is broken (e.g., all responses are rate-limits). Set a timeout on the test (e.g., 5 seconds). Call the async function and verify it throws QuotaExhaustedError within the timeout, not hanging or looping forever. Verify the error is actually thrown, not lost in a promise chain.

behaviors.description: Requests issued concurrently from multiple async callers in the same process all draw from the same shared quota bucket.
behaviors.justification: Concurrency bugs require actual concurrent execution to expose. A Unit test with sequential mock calls won't reveal if: quota is accidentally duplicated per caller, quota updates aren't atomic, or event-loop ordering causes stale reads. Real async concurrency (multiple Promise chains executing simultaneously) is needed to catch these failures.
behaviors.test_description: Launch multiple concurrent calls to the async function using Promise.all() (e.g., 5 callers making 2 requests each). Mock the HTTP client to return quota info and track which caller sees which quota value. Verify: (1) all concurrent requests draw from the same quota bucket, (2) quota decrements are consistent across callers, (3) no duplicated quota per caller, (4) concurrent requests correctly queue when quota is exhausted.

behaviors.description: When multiple requests are waiting to be sent, they are dispatched in the order they were originally submitted.
behaviors.justification: Testing FIFO ordering requires actual async dispatch, not just queue logic. A Unit test of the queue could pass while the real implementation violates FIFO due to improper event-loop handling or async sequencing. Integration testing with actual concurrent request submission and dispatch exposes FIFO violations.
behaviors.test_description: Submit multiple async requests concurrently (e.g., 5 requests with distinct IDs or payloads). Mock the HTTP client to record the order of received requests. Verify requests are sent in the exact order they were submitted, even if some are retried or delayed due to quota exhaustion. For example: if request A is submitted before request B, even if A needs a retry and B is ready first, A must be sent in the next dispatch window.
